summary: McKenzie documents the first three days of building his first software business, spending about 18 hours coding and setting up infrastructure. He chose GoDaddy for hosting at $10.02 total, rationalizing that he'd break even after one sale. He evaluates payment processors, finding eSellerate's workflow impressive but slow, and discovers Payloadz as a cheaper alternative that works through PayPal. The post reveals his early thinking about conversion optimization — wanting to minimize checkout steps between the 'Buy now' click and credit card entry.
key_points: He launched his first micro-ISV with only $10.02 in hosting costs, deliberately minimizing upfront investment before proving demand
His instinct for conversion optimization was present from day one — every extra page in checkout is 'an opportunity for second thoughts'
He compared payment processors on effective percentage rates at different revenue tiers, showing analytical rigor even at the earliest stage
He rationalized the time investment by comparing it to World of Warcraft — framing entrepreneurship as a more productive use of leisure hours
money_quotes: So I'll be in the black after my first sale, if you assume my time is worth nothing since the 18 hours would otherwise have been spent on an unproductive addiction.
No sense spending serious money on the project before I have some indication that there are actual, living, not-related-to-me people who want to spend money on it.
I don't need a shopping cart — every page that pops up from the time someone clicks my 'Buy now' link is an opportunity for second thoughts.
I want to show the product, pricing, and start getting the credit card information immediately.
Paypal is a trusted name (ok, so maybe not THAT trusted) and people have (hopefully) purchased from one of their shopping carts before, reducing barriers to the sale.
key_insight: Even at the very beginning of his entrepreneurial career, McKenzie's instinct was to minimize friction in the purchase flow and keep costs near zero until customers proved demand — principles that would define his later writing on software business.
